guide.sub: Four decades of back-road stories, gathered into one trip through the Scenic City — the riverfront, the railroads, the mountain-town cafes, and the makers our hosts found along the way.
guide.metaNote: every one a real Crossroads segment
guide.mapLede: Every stop our cameras visited, plotted across the city.
guide.quote: “Come travel the highways and by-ways of Tennessee.”
guide.note: We hope this guide leads you to new memories, a hidden gem, or someplace you’ve always meant to visit. Thanks for making Tennessee Crossroads part of your journey.
guide.colophonHTML: Tennessee Crossroads is a Nashville PBS production · Each place began as a story on the show

print.coverCaption: The Chattanooga Choo Choo, Market Street

sections.title: Eat & Drink
sections.intro: Barbecue on a hillside, a century-old snack made famous by the moon, and the bakeries and cafes that keep the Scenic City fed.

stops.name: Hillbilly Willy's Bar-B-Q
stops.blurb: Behind every great barbecue story is an interesting family — and the folks at Hillbilly Willy's prove the rule. Susan Watson found the smoky hot spot worth crossing town for.

stops.name: Bea's Restaurant
stops.blurb: At Bea's, the lazy Susan does the passing and the table does the talking — dinner comes family-style, and nobody leaves hungry. Rob Wilds pulls up a chair.

stops.name: Purple Daisy Picnic Cafe
stops.blurb: Life's a picnic at this former St. Elmo gas station at the foot of Lookout Mountain, where Ken Wilshire found plate lunches and front-porch charm.

stops.name: Boathouse Rotisserie & Raw Bar
stops.blurb: A couple of old rowboats, a view of the water, and some of the finest casual dining on the banks of the Tennessee River. Ken Wilshire takes a seat at the Boathouse.

stops.name: Niedlov's Breadworks
stops.blurb: Ken Wilshire visits the Southside bakery whose hand-shaped loaves helped make Main Street a destination — proof some places really are better than sliced bread.

stops.name: City Cafe
stops.blurb: Ken Wilshire stops downtown for a nostalgic, diner-style dining experience — comfort food served with a generous side of Chattanooga character.

stops.name: Moon Pies
stops.blurb: The MoonPie — the only treat like it in the universe — has been made by Chattanooga Bakery for more than a century. Ken Wilshire asks for the moon and gets it.

sections.title: See & Explore
sections.intro: From an aquarium on the riverfront to the birthplace of the tow truck — the stops that show off the Scenic City's range.

stops.name: Tennessee Aquarium
stops.blurb: From mountain streams to the open ocean, the Tennessee Aquarium takes you on a water journey right on the riverfront — the anchor of Chattanooga's downtown revival.

stops.name: Hunter Museum of American Art
stops.blurb: Perched on a bluff above the Tennessee River, the Hunter holds one of the South's premier collections of American art — and brings it down the hill to the people.

stops.name: Songbirds Guitar Museum
stops.blurb: When New York curators went looking for a home for their rare guitar collection, Chattanooga got the call. Joe Elmore tours Songbirds, just steps from the Choo Choo.

stops.name: International Towing & Recovery Museum
stops.blurb: Chattanooga is the birthplace of the wrecker, and this one-of-a-kind Broad Street museum tells the story — gleaming vintage tow trucks and all. Joe Elmore takes the tour.

stops.name: National Medal of Honor Heritage Center
stops.blurb: Steps from the Aquarium, this center offers an up-close experience with American military heroes — and tells the Chattanooga story at the roots of the Medal of Honor.

stops.name: Chattanooga Zoo
stops.blurb: Wildlife from the Gombe Forest to the Himalayas, all in one Chattanooga afternoon. Susan Watson drops in on the zoo's newest furry friends.

stops.name: Bessie Smith Cultural Center
stops.blurb: Part museum, part living stage, the Bessie Smith Cultural Center keeps the Empress of the Blues — and the story of Black Chattanooga — front and center.

stops.name: EPB Holiday Windows
stops.blurb: Every holiday season, employees of Chattanooga's Electric Power Board turn downtown windows into dazzling displays — a tradition that catches grown-ups in the same magic as kids. Seasonal.
stops.addr: 10 W Martin Luther King Jr Blvd

stops.name: North Pole Limited
stops.blurb: Come Christmastime, the Tennessee Valley Railroad becomes the North Pole Limited — an all-aboard house call on Santa that kids remember for years. Seasonal.

sections.title: Stay a While
sections.intro: Pardon me, boys — the landmark that turned a big-band song into a place to spend the night.

stops.name: Chattanooga Choo Choo
stops.blurb: The 1909 train depot made famous by a big-band song found new life as a hotel and gathering place. Rob Wilds boards at the Chattanooga Choo Choo.

sections.title: Makers
sections.intro: Glass from fire, dresses from junkyard metal, art from concrete and thin air — Chattanooga's makers work in unlikely materials.

stops.name: Chris Mosey, Glass Blower
stops.blurb: Glass has been made for 3,500 years; Chris Mosey makes it feel brand new. Watch molten color take shape at his downtown Ignis Glass Studio.

stops.name: John Petrey, Artist
stops.blurb: Sculptor John Petrey tailors elegant dresses from the unlikeliest fabric — metal and materials rescued from the junkyard. Ken Wilshire visits his Main Street studio.

stops.name: Kem Alexander, Concrete Artist
stops.blurb: In Kem Alexander's hands, rough concrete becomes something uncommonly beautiful. Tammi Arender meets the Chattanooga artist who pours her art in slabs.

stops.name: Ron Lowery, Photographer
stops.blurb: Ron Lowery combines a love of flying with a passion for photography, capturing Tennessee's rivers and ridges from the open air. You may never see them the same way again.
